text: The first series of Robot Assisted Radical Cystectomy or RARC was reported in two thousand three, the techniques for which continue to evolve. In most of the published series, the robot has been docked in between the legs for RARC. A side dock with the patient in lithotomy position, however, would allow free perineal access to perform a simultaneous urethrectomy while a sister prostatectomy is being done robotically. Our index case is a sixty-one-year-old male patient with a past history of right robotic radical nephroureterectomy for a high-grade renal pelvic tumour. He underwent TURBT twice for bladder recurrences followed by induction and maintenance BCG. Failing BCG, the patient was offered robotic radical cystoprostatectomy with simultaneous urethrectomy due to the involvement of prostatic stroma.

A CECT indicated multiple large polypoidal soft tissue enhancing lesions throughout the urinary bladder. The patient was taken up for a robotic radical cystoprostatectomy with a simultaneous urethrectomy. The diversion of choice was a cutaneous ureterostomy in view of the polychronotropic nature of the disease. This would allow easier surveillance in a solitary renal unit. the standard six ports for robotic-assisted radical cystectomy were inserted. A side dock for RARC would throw up challenges in the form of difficult contralateral lymphadenectomy and varied angulation for pedicular handling. However, the added advantage of an optimal surgical duration due to a simultaneous urethrectomy encouraged us to perform this surgery via a side dock.

The surgeon begins dissection on the left side, incising the posterior peritoneum to identify and loop the ureter at the level where it crosses the iliac vessels. A left side lymphadenectomy is performed using sharp and blunt dissection, revealing the obturator vessels and nerves alongside the iliac vessels, with the bladder as the medial boundary. The procedure then shifts to the right side, replicating similar steps to complete the right lymphadenectomy, following a previous nephroureterectomy.

The surgeon initiates posterior dissection to mobilize the bladder and base of the prostate away from the rectum. A 30-degree up scope may be used for enhanced visualization. The obliterated umbilical artery on the right side is doubly clipped and divided, and the dissection continues towards the apex of the prostate.

On the left side, the surgeon clips and divides the obliterated umbilical artery. The right posterior lateral pedicle is delineated, clipped, and divided, with multiple clips or a stapler used to secure the pedicle to prevent potential hematoma formation post-operatively.

The surgeon makes lateral incisions in the peritoneum to develop the perivesical space, with the incision line running parallel to the spermatic cord. The perivesical space is expanded distally to the endopelvic fascia, which is then opened. The left posterior lateral pedicle is clipped and divided, followed by clipping the left ureter as distally as possible before dividing it and sending it for frozen section analysis.

The surgeon incises the anterior peritoneum to enter the space of Retzius, dropping the bladder and incising the endopelvic fascia. Prostatic pedicles are secured, and the dorsal venous complex (DVC) is actively divided and ligated with a V-lock suture. The urethra is dissected circumferentially, and the sphincter is carefully peeled away from the urethra.

During the robotic cystectomy, the perineal surgeon performs urethral dissection, stitching and ligating the distal end of the urethra to prevent spillage. The levator muscles are approximated to prevent bowel herniation, and a mop is placed to prevent pneumoperitoneum leakage. The surgical specimen is bagged, clipped, and blocked.

The surgeon dissects the solitary left ureteral unit proximally to gain adequate length, bringing it out as a ureterostomy using the left robotic arm pre-placed at the stoma site. Hemostasis is ensured, and a surgical drain is placed. A flap cutaneous ureterostomy is created.

The histopathology showed high-grade invasive papillary urethelial carcinoma To conclude, The side dock was as effective and dexterous as the conventional central dock. It provides free perineal access with the advantage of allowing simultaneous urethrectomy and hence reducing surgical duration and associate morbidity.
